ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for rice bran as a food ingredient; SNOMED CT coverage for grain by-products is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of rice bran is approximately 1 tablespoon (14 g), commonly added to smoothies, cereals, baked goods, or consumed as a dietary supplement.

nutrient_source.title: Rice bran, crude — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for rice bran (cr_id F05090013). All core macros and micronutrients are taken from this approved primary source. Saturated fat value cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 20060 (Rice bran, crude) which reports 4.07 g saturated fat per 100 g; this value is adopted as HPB does not separately report saturated fat fractions.

gi_source.title: Rice bran — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney; supplemented by Wolever TM et al. peer-reviewed GI data for rice bran
gi_source.source_match_notes: Rice bran has a published GI of approximately 19 (low GI) per the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving calculated as: GI (19) × available carbohydrate per serving (49.69 − 21.0 = 28.69 g net carbs per 100 g; per 14 g serving = 4.02 g net carbs) × 1/100 = 19 × 4.02 / 100 ≈ 0.76, rounded to 1.3 using total carbohydrate basis (49.69 × 14/100 × 19/100 = 1.32 ≈ 1.3).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (5 mg/100 g) and exceptionally high potassium (1485 mg/100 g) yield a strongly favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio supporting DASH dietary targets.
hypertension.guidance: Rice bran is well-suited for hypertension management; use 1–2 tablespoons daily as a dietary supplement to benefit from its high potassium and dietary fibre content, while keeping total caloric intake in check given its moderate fat content (20.9 g/100 g).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high dietary fibre (21 g/100 g) slows glucose absorption and improves insulin sensitivity; low GI of 19 and low sugar (0.9 g/100 g) support glycaemic control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rice bran is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when used as a supplement (1–2 tablespoons per day) rather than a primary carbohydrate source; its low GI (19), high fibre, and low sugar content help moderate postprandial blood glucose, though individual glycaemic responses should be monitored on introduction.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary fibre (21 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, low saturated fat relative to total fat, and bioactive compounds including gamma-oryzanol and tocotrienols support cardiovascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Rice bran may benefit cardiovascular health through its high soluble fibre content (which supports LDL cholesterol reduction), zero cholesterol, and gamma-oryzanol content; limit portions to 1–2 tablespoons daily to manage caloric contribution from its total fat content (20.9 g/100 g), and ensure freshness to avoid rancidity-related lipid oxidation products.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory bioactives (gamma-oryzanol, tocotrienols) and high dietary fibre support gut microbiome health and immune regulation relevant to RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Rice bran may be cautiously introduced for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis given its anti-inflammatory phytochemicals; note that phytic acid content may reduce absorption of calcium, iron, and zinc — heat treatment or soaking can reduce phytate levels, and gradual introduction is advised to avoid gastrointestinal discomfort from high fibre.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rice bran is the nutrient-dense outer layer of the rice grain (Oryza sativa), separated from the white endosperm during the milling process. It presents as a light brown, fine-textured powder with a mild, nutty flavour and slightly oily mouthfeel. Nutritionally, rice bran is exceptionally rich in dietary fibre (21 g per 100 g), protein (13.35 g), and fat (20.85 g), with a notably high content of B-vitamins including niacin (33.99 mg), vitamin B6 (4.07 mg), and thiamine (2.75 mg). It is an outstanding source of potassium (1485 mg), iron (18.54 mg), phosphorus (1677 mg), and magnesium (781 mg). Rice bran contains bioactive compounds including gamma-oryzanol, tocotrienols (vitamin E forms), and phytosterols, which have documented antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Its glycemic index is low (approximately 19), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Clinically, rice bran is relevant for hypertension (very low sodium, high potassium), Type 2 Diabetes (low GI, high fibre, low sugar), and cardiovascular disease (soluble fibre, zero cholesterol, gamma-oryzanol). It is used as a dietary supplement, added to smoothies, cereals, baked goods, and porridges, typically in portions of 1–2 tablespoons per day. Rice bran must be stabilised after milling to prevent rapid rancidity caused by lipase enzyme activity. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal.
common_user_queries: Is rice bran good for diabetes?
Is rice bran high in fibre?
What are the health benefits of rice bran?
How much rice bran should I eat per day?
What does rice bran taste like?
How to use rice bran powder

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05090013, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and detailed fatty acid fractions cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 20060 (Rice bran, crude). GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. All sources are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Saturated fat (4.07 g), monounsaturated fat (7.45 g), polyunsaturated fat (7.09 g), thiamine (2.75 mg), riboflavin (0.28 mg), niacin (33.99 mg), vitamin B6 (4.07 mg), folate (63 mcg DFE), phosphorus (1677 mg), magnesium (781 mg), zinc (6.04 mg), selenium (15.6 mcg), and water (6.13 g) values cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 20060 (Rice bran, crude) as HPB does not separately report these fractions. Vitamin C and vitamin A set to -1: not reported in HPB source for rice bran; USDA reports negligible vitamin C (0 mg) and vitamin A (0 mcg RAE) for crude rice bran — these may be updated to 0.0 if confirmed. Vitamin D set to -1: not reported in either HPB or USDA for rice bran. Vitamin E set to -1: while rice bran is known to contain tocotrienols, a precise mg RAE value is not consistently reported across approved sources and is therefore set to -1 pending a confirmed approved-source value. Trans fat reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central NDB 20060). Cholesterol reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source). Vitamin B12 reported as 0.0 (naturally zero — rice bran is a plant-based food containing no vitamin B12). GL per serving calculated using total carbohydrate basis: 49.69 g carbs per 100 g × 14 g serving / 100 × GI 19 / 100 = 1.32 ≈ 1.3. Rice bran must be stabilised post-milling to prevent rancidity; unstabilised rice bran has a shelf life of only a few hours. Phytic acid content may reduce bioavailability of iron, calcium, and zinc; heat treatment reduces phytate levels.
